perturbation.original_text: The parties shall promptly review the Patent Review List and, in good-faith, discuss and negotiate during the Review Period whether any of the Patents listed on such Patent Review List (each, a "Shared Review Patent") should be or should have been, as applicable, an Assigned Patent, based upon the general guideline and principle that Shared Review Patents that are primarily paid for, primarily used by, or primarily arising out of, or for which associated costs and fees were primarily allocated to, the Business or any Acquired Company (as compared with the Seller Business) should be or should have been, as applicable, Assigned Patents.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties may, at their convenience, review the Patent Review List and discuss whether any of the Patents listed on such Patent Review List (each, a "Shared Review Patent") should be or should have been, as applicable, an Assigned Patent. The seller is not obligated to assign patents, even if primarily paid for, used by, or arising out of the Business or any Acquired Company.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'good faith' clause allows the seller to act in bad faith or delay tactics, frustrating the agreement's purpose. This alteration also makes the assignment of patents discretionary, contradicting the implied duty of cooperation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing under Delaware Law
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the requirements for prompt review and good-faith negotiation, and by stating the seller is not obligated to assign patents, the modification enables the seller to potentially frustrate the purchaser's expected benefits under the agreement, thus violating the implied covenant.

General Provisions Part 3 Territorial Applicability and General Rules Â§ 1-301. Territorial applicability; partiesâ power to choose applicable law. (a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this State and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this State or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), the Uniform Commercial Code applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) Section 2-402; (2) Sections 2A-105 and 2A-106; (3) Section 4-102; (4) Section 4A-507; (5) Section 5-116; (6) Section 8-110; (7) Sections 9-301 through 9-307; (8) Section 12-107. 5A Del. (b) The obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness, and care prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement. The parties, by agreement, may determine the standards by which the performance of those obliga
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original contract by removing the 'good faith' clause and making the assignment of patents discretionary. This directly undermines the implied duty of cooperation, potentially frustrating the purchaser's expected benefits. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically Â§ 1-302(b), states that 'The obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness, and care prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement.' By removing the 'good faith' requirement, the modified text attempts to disclaim this obligation, creating a legal contradiction. The original text imposes a duty to negotiate in good faith, which is now removed.

perturbation.original_text: Purchaser agrees that the quality of all Seller Excluded Products and any other products and services marketed or sold by the Purchaser Licensees under the Licensed Trademarks will conform to at least the level of quality of the Enterprise Products as currently provided by the Seller Entities as of the Initial Closing Date.
perturbation.changed_text: Purchaser agrees that the quality of all Seller Excluded Products and any other products and services marketed or sold by the Purchaser Licensees under the Licensed Trademarks may, at the sole discretion of the Purchaser, conform to the level of quality deemed acceptable by the Purchaser.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the quality control clause removes the standard requiring the quality of products marketed under licensed trademarks to match that of the seller's enterprise products. This could constitute trademark abandonment if the quality declines significantly.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification allows the Purchaser to lower the quality of products sold under the licensed trademarks. Under the Lanham Act, a trademark owner must maintain control over the quality of goods or services sold under the trademark. Failure to do so can result in a finding of abandonment, jeopardizing the trademark's validity. This change allows for potential quality decline and therefore potential abandonment, contradicting trademark law.

Construction and definitions; intent of chapter In the construction of this chapter, unless the contrary is plainly apparent from the context- The United States includes and embraces all territory which is under its jurisdiction and control. The word "commerce" means all commerce which may lawfully be regulated by Congress. The term "principal register"
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts trademark law, specifically the Lanham Act, by allowing the Purchaser to potentially lower the quality of products sold under the licensed trademarks. This contradicts the Lanham Act's requirement for trademark owners to maintain control over the quality of goods or services sold under the trademark. The original text ensured quality control. The changed text allows for quality decline, which could be seen as trademark abandonment under the Lanham Act. Snippet 1 refers to the Trademark Act of 1946, which includes sections relevant to trademark use and control. Snippet 2 references 15 USC 1127, which relates to construction and definitions within trademark law, reinforcing the legal basis for quality control requirements.
